issue_title: Delete Pipfile.lock as late as possible
issue_body:
When updating Pipfile.lock, the old version is deleted before anything happens.  If the update process fails (maybe because one Ctrl+C's it because of a mistake, or it's [taking too long][3], or [it crashes][4], etc.), then one is left with no lockfile.

Could the behaviour be changed so that the [lockfile deletion][1] is performed **immediately** before the [serialisation of the new content][2]?  (FWIW, I'd be happy to attempt a PR for this.)

I guess two rationales for the current behaviour:

1. Your `Pipfile.lock` should be in Git.  Yes, but it still sucks if you're doing a sequence of new package installs/etc. and haven't committed your work.
2. The update of the `Pipfile.lock` should be consistent with the current state of the virtualenv.  I'd buy that, but the current behaviour (no lockfile) doesn't strike me as better :wink:.

[1]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/core.py#L1026
[2]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/core.py#L1173
[3]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1785
[4]: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1837

------------

##### Steps to replicate

1. Start with an existing Pipenv environment (i.e. you have a working `Pipfile`/`Pipfile.lock`).
2. Run `pipenv install <any_dep_that_takes_a_long_time>`.
3. Hit Ctrl+C before it's finished.

issue_title: Clarify docs and help messages on cli commands
issue_body:
Excited pipenv user here. I don't understand what several of the commands do. Here is the output of `pipenv`:

```
Commands:
  check      Checks for security vulnerabilities and against PEP 508 markers
             provided in Pipfile.
  clean      Uninstalls all packages not specified in Pipfile.lock.
  graph      Displays currently–installed dependency graph information.
  install    Installs provided packages and adds them to Pipfile, or (if none
             is given), installs all packages.
  lock       Generates Pipfile.lock.
  open       View a given module in your editor.
  run        Spawns a command installed into the virtualenv.
  shell      Spawns a shell within the virtualenv.
  sync       Installs all packages specified in Pipfile.lock.
  uninstall  Un-installs a provided package and removes it from Pipfile.
  update     Runs lock, then sync.
```

1. `lock` "Generates Pipfile.lock." Is it doing that based on the current environment, or based on what's specified in the Pipfile, or some other thing? Sometimes it seems to be accessing the internet, which is altogether unexpected.

2. Does `open` open the editor with the virtualenv bin directory prepended to the PATH? Or something else? 

3. `install` "Installs provided packages". What are "provided packages"? Do they include the ones in my Pipfile? If no arguments are given, it installs "all" packages; which "all" are you talking about: in the Pipfile, Pipfile.lock, current active environment? 

4. Which of these commands modify my environment? Which modify my Pipfile? Which modify my Pipfile.lock?



Could the cli help text and documentation clear this up, please?

Thank you for all your work on pipenv.

enum34 should be required

enum34 is not required

issue_title: Unable to parse --no-binary when installing psycopg2
issue_body:
Unable to install psycopg2 with option `--no-binary`. Might be related to this issue? https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/870

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: Mac OS 10.13.3
1. Python version: Python 2.7.10
1. Pipenv version: pipenv, version 9.0.3

##### Expected result
Describe what you expected.
`pipenv install "--no-binary psycopg2 psycopg2"` --> `pip install --no-binary psycopg2 psycopg2`

##### Actual result
$ pipenv install "--no-binary psycopg2 psycopg2" --verbose
Installing --no-binary psycopg2 psycopg2…
⠋Installing '--no-binary psycopg2 psycopg2'
WARNING: Invalid requirement, parse error at "'--no-bin'"
ABORTING INSTALL... You will have to reinstall any packages that failed to install.
You may have to manually run pipenv lock when you are finished.

##### Steps to replicate
$ pipenv --three
$ pipenv install "--no-binary psycopg2 psycopg2"

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Python AWS lambda packages require their dependencies to be packaged with the source, which is suggested to be done using `pip install -t <package` (see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-python-how-to-create-deployment-package.html). This means you can't use pipenv directly to manage their dependencies, because it doesn't support the `-t/--target` option.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
`pipenv install -t path/to/src <package>` installs a dependency into `Pipfile/Pipfile.lock`, and runs `pip install -t path/to/src <package>`.

I think it'd make sense not to do the equivalent of `pipenv install <package>` at the same time, ie it'd be like only running `pip -t` and match its behaviour. Just the dependency would end up managed by pipenv.

Note that pip does't have a `pip uninstall -t`, I think it'd be okay not to have a `pipenv uninstall -t` also (though `pipenv uninstall <package>` should still remove it from `Pipfile`).

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
My current workaround is to use `pipenv lock -r > requirements.txt` and then `pip install -r requirements.txt -t path/to/src`.

**Additional context**
I assume this would depend on a fix to https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/2444, because on MacOS `pip install -t <package>` requires a setup.cfg to be added, so `pipenv -t` would be the same - see that AWS link for details on that.

When installing pylint with python 3.6.6 dependencies could not be resolved to lock.
On Centos 7, Python 2.7.5,  pipenv, version 2018.7.1, pylint was installed without any issues.

One of the concerns raised in previous discussions of this idea is that it risks inheriting all the problems that have historically plagued setup.py, such as having to execute arbitrary code just to extract project metadata.

Having `Pipfile.lock` mitigates a large part of that problem (since the generated JSON metadata will already be static at install time), and a gemspec-style `pyproject()` directive would make it straightforward to delegate to a library-style `pyproject.toml` file when a given repo supports both direct deployment as a service and publication as a versioned component.

However, it would also be possible to explicitly discourage (but not entirely prevent) the inclusion of arbitrary control flow logic by blocking access to most of the builtins when executing `Pipfile` code. For example:

    >>> limited_namespace = dict(__builtins__={})
    >>> exec("import sys", limited_namespace, {})
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    ImportError: __import__ not found

This wouldn't provide a security boundary (sandboxing Python code isn't that simple), but it *would* mean that imports and the `open()` command wouldn't work by default, and folks would have to actively work to gain access to the standard builtins in order to change that.

First of all I think this initiative is amazing and really hope this will make portable Linux binaries trivial.

I've had some experience with this on the company I work for. Basically we came up with the same solution, except that we compile under a CentOS 5 chroot.

But one of the problems we faced was **libstdc++**. We also use **gcc 4.8.5**, which means the libstdc++ version is 3.4.19.

CentOS default libstdc++ is 3.4.12, therefore if you are compiling C++ code using this approach, you might have to run the executable only on CentOS 7 and earlier.

So the most obvious solution would be to just ship ```libstdc++```. This is what we did, but then we got missing symbols on recent Linux distributions because they have a libstdc++ newer than 3.4.19, and some crucial system libraries (specially OpenGL implementations) were linked against it.

Not the most beautiful solution, but we ended up having to create an initialization script that would check which version is newer (the shipped or system one), and set LD_LIBRARY_PATH to the shipped one if necessary.

I know that most of base libraries are written in C, but even though I think this is an important issue, specially if someone is using OpenGL (I know that at least Mesa and Nvidia proprietary implementation use C++).

So have anyone else thought about this problem?

issue_title: Use KleinResource, or a subset thereof, in the error handling pipeline

issue_title: GridFS ensure indexes is done without yielding
issue_body:
https://github.com/twisted/txmongo/blob/master/txmongo/_gridfs/__init__.py#L55

When instanciating a `GridFS` object, an async call to mongo is done to create the chunks collection indexes.
The trouble is this call is never waited so this can lead to errors if the database is disconnected at the wrong time. One solution I see to solve this would be to store a defer on the ensure_indexes call in the GridFS

```python
>>> gfs = GridFS(db)
>>> # playing with gridfs
>>> yield gfs.ensure_indexes_defer
>>> db.disconnect()
```

Another smaller concern is on the indexes themselves, according to [mongodb's documentation](https://docs.mongodb.org/manual/core/gridfs/#gridfs-indexes):
> GridFS uses indexes on each of the chunks and files collections for efficiency. Drivers that conform to the GridFS specification automatically create these indexes for convenience. You can also create any additional indexes as desired to suit your application’s needs.

So I think txmongo should create indexes for the `fs.files` collection as well

```python
    def erase_in_display(self, how=0, private=False):
        """Erases display in a specific way.
        :param int how: defines the way the line should be erased in:
            * ``0`` -- Erases from cursor to end of screen, including
              cursor position.
            * ``1`` -- Erases from beginning of screen to cursor,
              including cursor position.
            * ``2`` -- Erases complete display. All lines are erased
              and changed to single-width. Cursor does not move.
        :param bool private: when ``True`` character attributes are left
                             unchanged **not implemented**.
        """
        if how == 0:
            # a) erase from cursor to the end of the display, including
            #    the cursor,
            interval = range(self.cursor.y + 1, self.lines)
        elif how == 1:
            # b) erase from the beginning of the display to the cursor,
            #    including it,
            interval = range(self.cursor.y)
        elif how == 2:
            # c) erase the whole display.
            interval = range(self.lines)

        for line in interval:
            self.buffer[line][:] = \
                (self.cursor.attrs for _ in range(self.columns))

        # In case of 0 or 1 we have to erase the line with the cursor.
        if how == 0 or how == 1:
            self.erase_in_line(how)
```

There is a potential bug here, and it will be triggered while `how` in the [0, 1, 2].

issue_title: PycurlClient does not support TCP keep-alive
issue_body:
The RequestsClient uses a session with which one is able to get TCP keep-alive by creating a single client per process:

`stripe.default_http_client = stripe.http_client.RequestsClient()`

After the initial stripe API handshake, the API calls can be completed much quicker. For long running processes (say a web-application) this yields a great speed up. In our case it cuts the costs of API calls in half.

Doing the same thing with the PycurlClient doesn't work however. The following setup will still discard the connection after every single request:

`stripe.default_http_client = stripe.http_client.PycurlClient()`

To facilitate this the Curl instance would have to be kept around if I'm not mistaken. So basically the PycurlClient might want to use curl as a kind of session, just like the RequestsClient does:

```python
class PycurlClient(object):
    def __init__(..., curl=None):
        self.curl = curl or pycurl.Curl()
    def request(...):
        curl = self.curl
```

```

and the repository exists.

If instead we do:

I suggest updating the contribute page, unless I am the only one experiencing this, or maybe I am missing another undocumented step?

issue_title: Add FieldMeta details to API docs

issue_body:
This super issue covers the concerted effort to get NuPIC Python and C++ working on Windows.

Relocation of the [SWIG](http://www.swig.org/) wrapper and bindings, has isolated remaining Windows Python porting tasks to this Core library. 

In summary, these two goals need to be achieved -
1. Automated NuPIC build systems can download and bind to all versions of the core library.
2. The Windows NuPIC Core deployment package is compatible with Windows NuPIC.

Relates to "Windows binary build and deployment" https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1786

* * *

The currently planned route forward is;
- [x] **1.** [Finish the current C++ extension work.](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/566)
- [x] **2.** [Python 3.5 support.](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/567)
- [x] **3.** [MinGW GCC support.](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/568)
- [x] **4.** [LLVM and Clang support.](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/613)
- [x] **5.** [Finalize Windows deployment](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/571) - Current task

All items rely on SWIG and binding work being complete, or close to. With the first item requiring A few more days to wrangle the build, and determine whether this is a dead-end. 

The second route has some uncertainty over how long 3.5 support in a 2.7 code base will take. The Core library is virtually ready. And with Austin's investigation we should be able to judge how long this could be. Only 2to3 and six changes in NuPIC Python?  Uncertainty over 2to3 timing and ongoing support. Existing installed 3.x Python needs rebuilding with VS2015? Code review changes?  

The third item tries a more recent TDM-GCC package with script ideas from https://github.com/develersrl/gccwinbinaries  This should be a simple change to the Core lib building. Will need Travis and AppVeyor scripts and Yaml updating, taking into account CI automated build limits (40 mins maximum on AppVeyor). It didn't take too long, before the C++11 adoption, to update the Core and NuPIC to use the correct VC compiler and Window's SDK. Getting to a point of needing MySQL setup properly for some of the tests. Adding the compiler is straight forward, adding the appropriate SDK may take longer. Similar to route 1 there may still be issues and a possible need to build and supply Python from source.

* * *

A C++0X compiled Core library can be used with Python 2.7 (x64), as was demonstrated a while back https://gist.github.com/rcrowder/5fd1e964bf1bd6881c34 But with the adoption of more C++11 language features, there became a need to use more recent Visual C compilers. But doing so broke the ability to compiler extensions for Python 2.7 on Windows.

It looks possible to use various tricks and tweaks to get a build working using a mix of compilers and SDKs. But first see Piotr Dobrogost reply here http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3047542/building-lxml-for-python-2-7-on-windows/5122521#5122521 and a more detailed discussion here http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2484511/can-i-use-visual-studio-2010s-c-compiler-with-visual-studio-2008s-c-runtim?lq=1

Most seem to advocate dropping language features to use older compilers, moving away from certain C++11 features. That is possible but impracticable for NuPIC. Adding support for GCC and clang seems a likely next route to take. Visual C compilers have been lagging behind with certain C++11 features, with clang having the most features covered (so a favorite to move to over MinGW/Cygwin GCC). An alternative answer within the former linked SO article replies, is to look into Cython extensions. A risky task of unknown size.

With the release of Python 3.5 seeing support for Visual Studio 2015, that opens a route that would solve this issue. But could lead to trying to adapt to 3.x only, or have mixed support of 2.7 and 3.x! (split repos?). Microsoft's efforts of late to support Python within the Visual Studio IDE do look great thou. https://www.visualstudio.com/en-us/features/python-vs.aspx https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLReL099Y5nRdLgGAdrb_YeTdEnd23s6Ff

Alternative Python packages exists such as WinPython, Anaconda, Python(x,y), etc. Most, if not all, relying on older 2010 or 2013 compiler versions. NuPIC Core relies on Visual Studio 2015, and the latest Visual C compilers, and so has similar build issues to what we currently face.

**TODO** -Format this super-issue and arrange sub-issues. Labels, Milestone, Assignees, Feedback, Guidelines and Markdown.

issue_title: Allow a global CORS policy configuration
issue_body:
It would be nice if there was a way to configure a global CORS policy for all resources. Although the documentation shows how an external object can be used to set the `cors_policy` for each resource (http://cornice.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html), it still doesn't seem very DRY. I currently have a large number of resources defined throughout my code and I will have to modify each one in order to support CORS. By providing a single point of configuration for all resources - or even a global default configuration - I think this would make dealing with CORS much nicer.

Something like this could be used in `main()`:

```python
config.route_prefix = '/api'
config.cors_policy = {'origins': ('*',), 'max_age': 42, 'credentials': True}

# Alternatively
config.cors_default_policy = {'origins': ('*',), 'max_age': 42, 'credentials': True}
```

Originally by *boltmann* on 2011-04-19

For the new module handling videos in Invenio ("BibEncode") we need support for task dependencies in BibSched. There should be a parameter upon task submission that defines an execution dependency on another task that is currently running. 

"BibEncode" has a daemon mode that launches multiple other "BibEncode" tasks for transcoding and other stuff defined by a submission.
Let us say that one of these tasks ("A") moves a video file and alters the metadata of the video.
Other "BibEncode" tasks ("B", "C") are going to trancode the video afterwards and extract thumbnails from it.
"B" and "C" cannot be executed before the videos was moved and altered by task "A". 
On submission of "B" and "C" a parameter should be settable that means "do not start before task "A" has been executed successfully".

One possible way would be to have the task resubmit itself in case that the task it depends upon was not yet successfully executed.

This is different from the the "postprocess" approach because multiple tasks, possibly in parallel, need to be run.

How to reproduce
=============

For example, in the Invenio demo site, click on any file (with URL like `http://localhost:8888/record/59/files/0204143.pdf`)

to get:

![screenshot 2015-05-26 22 18 05](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/580727/7822346/2151b2fa-03f5-11e5-8190-19f35951ffd1.png)


Same happens if a new file is uploaded through FFT and then tries to be accessed. The metadata reflects the change as field 856 is created (and also the file is listed in `bibdocfile` cli) but it is not accessible in the URL.

issue_title: docker: demo site building and local vs remote JSON schema access
issue_body:
When building Invenio 3 demo site [using docker](http://invenio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation/installation-quick.html#using-docker), the system does not pass the JSON Schema validation step:

```
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jsonschema/validators.py", line 122, in validate
    for error in self.iter_errors(*args, **kwargs):
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jsonschema/validators.py", line 98, in iter_errors
    for error in errors:
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jsonschema/_validators.py", line 199, in ref
    scope, resolved = validator.resolver.resolve(ref)
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jsonschema/validators.py", line 336, in resolve
    return url, self._remote_cache(url)
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/functools32/functools32.py", line 400, in wrapper
    result = user_function(*args, **kwds)
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jsonschema/validators.py", line 346, in resolve_from_url
    raise RefResolutionError(exc)
jsonschema.exceptions.RefResolutionError: HTTPConnectionPool(host='127.0.0.1', port=80): Max retries exceeded with url: /schema/marc21/bibliographic/bd-v1.0.0.json (Caused by NewConnectionError('<requests.packages.urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x7fbd19946dd0>: Failed to establish a new connection: [Errno 111] Connection refused',))
```

The web container does not have access to the nginx container.

Ideally, the schemas can be checked locally, not needing the HTTP access. This is connected to `JSONSCHEMAS_HOST` settings that use 127.0.0.1 when running via docker:

```console
$ git grep SCHEMA scripts/
scripts/create-instance.sh:JSONSCHEMAS_CONF=`cat <<EOF
scripts/create-instance.sh:JSONSCHEMAS_ENDPOINT='/schema'
scripts/create-instance.sh:JSONSCHEMAS_HOST='http://${INVENIO_WEB_HOST}'
scripts/create-instance.sh:echo "${JSONSCHEMAS_CONF}" >> ../../var/${INVENIO_WEB_INSTANCE}-instance/${INVENIO_WEB_INSTANCE}.cfg
scripts/populate-instance.sh:SCHEMA="http://${INVENIO_WEB_HOST}/schema/marc21/bibliographic/bd-v1.0.0.json"
scripts/populate-instance.sh:demouuids=$(dojson -i $demomarc21pathname -l marcxml do marc21 schema ${SCHEMA} | \
```

(IIRC I tried to use `localhost` in the past but it wasn't recognised either.)

One option would be to try to supply real IP address, but the best would be to simply recognise addresses such as `0.0.0.0`, `127.0.0.1`, `localhost` as being local, and do the schema validation in Python, not having to access the schemas over the web at all.

P.S. Centralising the ticket here, but we can solve it in the appropriate repository, depending on the chosen method.

First thanks for such a convenient tool to get data from all over the world.
But I met this problem when I tried to get daily historical data of Crude Oil from Yahoo,
my code is like this:

import pandas_datareader.data as data
import datetime
begin = datetime.datetime(2017,1,1)
end = datetime.datetime(2017,3,31)
oil = data.DataReader("CL.NYM", 'yahoo', begin, end)
oil.head()

issue_title: Undefined behavior while running alice29.txt test with clang-5 sanitizers
issue_body:
I believe this `load of misaligned address` to be undefined behavior. To replicate, compile brotli with bleading edge clang, throw in some sanitizers for good measure:
```
export CFLAGS="-O2 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined -fsanitize-coverage=trace-pc-guard -fno-sanitize-recover=all -fsanitize=float-divide-by-zero -fsanitize=float-cast-overflow"
export CXXFLAGS="-O2 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined -fsanitize-coverage=trace-pc-guard -fno-sanitize-recover=all -fsanitize=float-divide-by-zero -fsanitize=float-cast-overflow"
export LDFLAGS="-O2 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined -fsanitize-coverage=trace-pc-guard -fno-sanitize-recover=all -fsanitize=float-divide-by-zero -fsanitize=float-cast-overflow"
export CC=/third_party/llvm-build/Release+Asserts/bin/clang
export CXX=/third_party/llvm-build/Release+Asserts/bin/clang++

issue_title: "Create Tearsheet" Functions - Lack of Clarity: long_short vs. demeaned
issue_body:
In working on issue #167, I noticed a lack of clarity in regards to the use of the `long_short` vs. `demeaned` flags in some of the functions. The input parameters of the "create tearsheet" functions in the tears module contains the `long_short` parameter. This bool parameter is then used within those functions as the demean input to functions like `perf.mean_return_by_quantile()`. 

In my understanding, the `demeaned` argument is used to basically subtract each return by the mean universe return. This is different than when it is used in the call to `perf.factor_returns()` which factor weights the returns. If `long_short` is True, then the factor "values" themselves, not the returns, are demeaned during the weighting process.

I have thought of two possible ways of dealing with this:

1. We could modify the doc string so that the `long_short` parameter description more accurately describes this issue. Something like: 
``` python
"""
long_short : bool
        Should this computation happen on a long short portfolio? If so, then factor values
        will be demeaned across the factor universe when factor weighting the portfolio. 
        Additionally, mean quantile returns will be demeaned across the factor universe.
"""
```
2. We could create a separate `demeaned` argument for these "create tearsheet" functions.

issue_title: Sentry is noisy on process exit
issue_body:
We are using Sentry to report errors from cron tasks. We have `MAILTO` set as a backup so that we receive emails about critical errors which happen before raven has a chance to initialize.

This leads to us getting the occasional email containing:

    Sentry is attempting to send 1 pending error messages
    Waiting up to 10 seconds
    Press Ctrl-C to quit

It happens because there's a [hardcoded `initial_timeout`](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/69a005b19ff5a6ad521a342689f7634a69e12049/raven/transport/threaded.py#L63) of 0.1 seconds in the process exit hook. Is there any chance you could make this timeout configurable the way `shutdown_timeout` is?

issue_title: raven causing odd return value for traceback.format_exc()?
issue_body:
While using `raven-python`, we got a few errors recently suggesting that Python's `traceback.format_exc()` returned a strange value when the error was reported to Sentry. The value looked like the following in the Sentry UI (with the single quote on the second line):

    'NoneType
    '

I see lots of issues in this repo mentioning `NoneType`, so I thought I'd ask if you think raven is the culprit here.

Below are more details.

We're using raven 5.23.0, Django 1.9.7, and Python 3.5.2.

Because of issue #550 (exception chaining), we added a custom `SENTRY_CLIENT` that simply adds the value of `traceback.format_exc()` to the `extra` dict inside `captureException()`. We are doing this to get the full exception chain.

This has always worked fine, except in the case I'm reporting here.  A few times when running a custom management command, the Sentry UI shows the above `NoneType` value under "Additional Data" (where the extra dict is displayed), instead of the text of an exception chain.

Since the exception chain didn't display, I don't know what caused the issue, and I'm not sure if it's reproducible yet.

Does `raven-python` do anything that might interfere with the return value of `traceback.format_exc()`? Thanks.

Hello!
Right now the 'plugins.stores.sparqlstore' module has a big warning saying "The SPARQL Store does not support blank-nodes!" because they can't be expressed in SPARQL.

Playing with the 4Store triple store, I've found that it does actually let you represent blank nodes in SPARQL by using a special URI form, like this: `<bnode:b000012>`

In the process of getting the rdflib-web module to browse a 4Store triple store using the 'plugins.stores.sparqlstore' module, I hacked support for these blank nodes into the code, and it seems to be working fine. Without this change, rdflib-web can't be used on my data, it raises an exception as soon as it starts due to there being blank nodes in the data.

I noticed that the sparqlstore constructor takes a 'bNodeAsURI' flag which is completely unused, I thought maybe this is what it's meant for so I used that as a way of enabling this functionality.

issue_title: sparql query with sub-query is not working as expected
issue_body:
Please see the example in https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#subqueries.

The result of the script below should resemble

```
y           minName
:bob     "B. Bar"
:carol   "C. Baz"
```

But we get null instead of `minName` values

```
[
    (rdflib.term.URIRef('http://people.example/bob'), None),
    (rdflib.term.URIRef('http://people.example/carol'), None)
]
```

The script

```
import rdflib
import io

data = """
@prefix : <http://people.example/> .

:alice :name "Alice", "Alice Foo", "A. Foo" .
:alice :knows :bob, :carol .
:bob :name "Bob", "Bob Bar", "B. Bar" .
:carol :name "Carol", "Carol Baz", "C. Baz" .
"""

graph = rdflib.Graph()

f = io.StringIO(data)
graph.parse(f, format='n3')

result = graph.query("""
PREFIX : <http://people.example/>
PREFIX : <http://people.example/>

SELECT ?y ?minName
WHERE {
  :alice :knows ?y .
  {
    SELECT ?y (MIN(?name) AS ?minName)
    WHERE {
      ?y :name ?name .
    } GROUP BY ?y
  }
}
""")

print(list(result))
```

issue_title: Allow some gradual degradation if font file is not loaded
issue_body:
Hi there,

during a deploy of a new customer page we noticed that all their windows PCs couldn't show font-awesome icons (and our own icon fonts) in all versions of IE. After much debugging, we noticed that this was because windows has a setting that disallows downloading of font files from the internet as a security measure - which is exactly what their admins have used to impose better 'security' on all of their workspace computers.

Now, this let to the unique situation that all the font css  files where loaded, but none of the actual fonts where. Which completely broke our application. 😠

Our workaround was, to redefine our own icon font to use unicode code points that at least have a sensible fallback on windows (to my knowledge the only platform with such a setting). (But ideally all platforms should be able to provide fallbacks).

So here is what I propose: change the unicode code points of FontAwesome to use code points that have at least a semantically identical meaning, so that some fallback is possible.

The reason being: I would much rather have a not so great looking website that is still usable by clients, where I can tell them that they can change this setting and make it look great again instead of a completely unusable site where I have to solve the problem of not running with web fonts, as the client is not accepting the broken page.

issue_body:
`normal_difference` calculates percentiles for the difference between normal distributions.

Since the difference between normal distributions is normally distributed this functionality should be covered by a transformation of the input to the difference distribution and passed onto `normal_percentiles`.

issue_title: Wronmg terminal width reported to bash prompt
issue_body:
I have a bash prompt defined in **~/.bashrc** as such:

PS1=$(printf '%*s\n' "${COLUMNS:-$(**tput cols**)}" '' | tr ' ' _)'\n\[\e[32m\]\u@\h\[\e[0m\] \[\e[33m\]\w\[\e[0m\] <\[\e[36m\]$(git symbolic-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null | cut -d"/" -f 3)$(svn info 2>/dev/null | sed -n "/URL:/s/.*\///p" | head -n 1 )\[\e[0m\]>\n> \[\e[33m\]\t\[\e[0m\] \[\e[36m\]\j \[\e[33m\]\s\[\e[0m\] \$ '

The result of "**tput cols**" at evaluation time of **~/.bashrc** when hitting **F12** seems to be **80**, afterwards "**tput cols**" returns the correct value (in my case 277). When launching a new bash instance within that existing ond, "tput cols" within evaluation of ~/.bashrc correctly returns 277. 

Maybe we have a bug upon first initializing a bash within guake at evaluation time of **~/.bashrc** (resulting in an ugly prompt in my case). Other terminal windows do not have that problem, even though launching with console widths > 80 chars.

issue_title: Automatically created Loops should be closed automatically
issue_body:
Constructor of ``msgpackrpc.Session`` class has an optional argument ``loop=None``.  When ``loop`` is not specified, new Loop will be automatically created in the constructor.

However, as automatically-created Loops are not closed automatically, it uses more file descriptors than expected.  This becomes an issue for clients that connects/disconnects frequently.
I think automatically-created Loops should be closed (i.e., ``session._loop._ioloop.close()``) when its associated ``Session`` is closed.

I have two ideas:

1. If ``Loop`` instance is not specified by user (i.e., automatically created in ``Session`` constructor), close the Loop when ``session.close()`` method is called.
2. Add ``close_loop=True`` to the ``Session`` constructor argument.   Close the Loop only if ``close_loop`` is set to True  when ``session.close()`` method is called.  This behavior is similar to [*closefd* option of io.FileIO](https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#io.FileIO).

In addition, it is better to add `close()` method to `msgpackrpc.Loop` class, to allow users to close the internal tornado IOLoop.

issue_title: Fiabilisation des paramètres OpenFisca pour l'allocation de solidarité spécifique (ASS)
issue_body: Ce besoin a été remonté lors de l'analyse des cas de test pour la revalorisation de l'ASS au 1er avril 2018 suite aux écarts/différences entres les paramètres fournis par l'outil de simulation des tests MSA et les paramètres dans OF.

### Problème
 
[Selon l'URSSAF:
](https://www.urssaf.fr/portail/home/employeur/beneficier-dune-exoneration/exonerations-ou-aides-liees-au-s/jeunes-entreprises-innovantes/regles-de-cumul.html
)
> Les exonérations JEI ne sont pas cumulables, pour l’emploi d’un même salarié, avec le bénéfice d’une aide à l’emploi de l’Etat, d’une autre mesure d’exonération totale ou partielle des cotisations patronales, de taux spécifiques, d’assiettes ou de montants forfaitaires de cotisations.

Ceci exclut donc les primes 1er salarié, la prime PME, l'exonération générale sur les bas salaires, et l'éxonéraiton des allocations familiales. Le CICE est lui compatible. 

Toujours selon l'URRSAF, 
> Une option entre mesures d’allègements est toutefois possible.
L’option en faveur de l’exonération jeune entreprise innovante s’effectue salarié par salarié et non au niveau de l’entreprise.

L'employeur peut donc choisir de privilégier l'exo JEI. Dans la pratique, étant donné les profils auxquels s'appliquent l'exo JEI, elle est la plupart du temps bien plus avantageuse que ces autres réductions, concentrées sur les bas salaires.

### Implémentation

On choisit donc de faire en sorte que **si le booléen `jeune_entreprise_innovante` est vérifié, les aides et réductions sont annulées**. 

L'idéal, dans un deuxième temps, serait d'afficher un message : 

>"Attention : vous avez choisi d'appliquer l'exonération de cotisations sociales employeur JEI pour votre employé, mais il serait plus avantageux pour vous de ne pas le faire pour bénéficier des réductions xxx"

No matching distribution found for OpenFisca-Core<13.0,>=12.1.1 (from OpenFisca-France==18.2.6)
```
How we can solve that?

In this bareme :

```
<BAREME code="apprentissage_add" >
            <TRANCHE code="tranche0">
              <SEUIL>
                <VALUE deb="2004-01-01" fin="X" valeur="0" />
              </SEUIL>

                <VALUE deb="2005-01-01" fin="2005-12-31" valeur="0.0012" />

if `X is < 2012-12-31`, the computed taux will be 0 (null)

issue_title: Symbolic exploration sometimes leads to incorrect results
issue_body:
### OS / Environment

Distributor ID:	ArchLinux
Description:	ArchLinux
Release:	rolling-release

### Manticore version

Last revision (465c926a2e39d5daf1405711dac61b6ddd0a2527)

### Python version

Python 2.7.13

### Summary of the problem

Using symbolic exploration leads to incorrect results. This means that sometimes a function returns an unexpected value.

### Step to reproduce the behavior

This is a python script to reproduce this issue:

```
from os.path import isdir
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM

cpus = 10
workspace = "ecrecover_out"
assert not isdir(workspace)
m = ManticoreEVM(procs=cpus, workspace_url=workspace)

m.verbosity(3) 
source_code = """
contract C {

    mapping(bytes32 => address) private ecrecover_;

    function ecrecover_reveal(bytes32 x, uint8 v, bytes32 r, bytes32 s, bytes32 hash, address y) returns (bool) {
        if (sha3(x, v, r, s) == hash) {
            if (ecrecover_[hash] == 0x0) {
                ecrecover_[hash] = y;
                return true;
                }
        }
        
        return false;
    }
}
"""

owner_account = m.create_account(balance=10000000)
print "[+] Creating a owner account", hex(owner_account)

user_account1 = m.create_account(balance=100000000)
print "[+] Creating a user account (1)", hex(user_account1)

user_account2 = m.create_account(balance=1000000000)
print "[+] Creating a user account (2)", hex(user_account2)

attacker_account = m.create_account(balance=1000000000)
print "[+] Creating an attacker account", hex(attacker_account)

print "[+] Creating a contract account", 
contract_account = m.solidity_create_contract(source_code, owner=owner_account)

print "[+] Adding user_account1 secret to ecrecover"
contract_account.ecrecover_reveal(0xe54f3efc1bb7ba885daa49e31a02da211b8a2f7e2a55902e3c9921660a61a85a, 27, 0x41, 0x42, 0xa31d8e991907f4c383e90d7f8236fd70f83f7a2564abf365506f7508d10e23cc, user_account1)

print "[+] Adding user_account2 secret to ecrecover"
contract_account.ecrecover_reveal(0xe54f3efc1bb7ba885daa49e31a02da211b8a2f7e2a55902e3c9921660a61a85a, 27, 0x61, 0x62, 0x50cd79dc4ad0dfa58d5f599051544128dcd0c52ff5acc0ad8a6fdeeba170043c, user_account2)

symbolic_data = m.make_symbolic_buffer(320)
symbolic_value = m.make_symbolic_value()
m.transaction(caller=attacker_account,
              address=contract_account,
              data=symbolic_data,
              value=symbolic_value )

m.finalize()
```
Just run this script and then use grep to look for the return values of ecrecover_reveal:

ecrecover_out/test_00000004.tx-return: 0

The latest release has a few changes. They are summarized in the [release notes](https://github.com/clab/dynet/releases/tag/2.0.1), but it would be helpful to have a document telling me just what I need to change in my 2.0-based code to upgrade to 2.0.1.

I am currently working through the examples to get familiar with this interesting library and I've noticed that currently the decode method of the attention network requires the output sequence as input.
Once it decodes a character, the decoder is fed the embedding of the last character in order to proceed.
Wouldn't this mean that the network is always being passed the correct output embedding (instead of the embedding of the character it actually predicted), and that it is therefore being "illegally" helped with the decoding?
I wonder if this actually influences generalization, because this behavior is only there for training.

issue_title: Let NEXRADLevel2File's filename be a gzipped (.gz) file
issue_body:
All of the level 2 archive files that I've seen from [NOAA's data archive site](http://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/nexradinv/) have been .gz files.  Would it be worth detecting the filename's extension, and if it is a .gz file opening it with `gzip.open` instead of just using open?  As it is, you can work around this by using an open .gz file as the filename.  I can submit a pull request if I need to; it will basically only add a couple lines in `NEXRADLevel2File.__init__`:

    if filename.endswith('.gz'):  # maybe should use os.path.splitext()[-1] == '.gz' to be cross-platform?
        fh = gzip.open(filename, 'rb') # 'rb' is the default, don't really need it...

and it would need to add the relevant imports, too.

As an aside, everywhere I've seen on NOAA's website mentions that the radar data is stored as a bzipped file, but all of the ones I've seen have been gzipped.  Granted, I've only worked with a single data set from KTLX.  Are the others different?

issue_title: Review the datetime helpers in gcloud._helpers.
issue_body:
`gcloud._helpers` has accumulated a collection of ~10 helper functions related to `datetime` handling. These could perhaps use a review to determine if they are all essential and maximally useful.

The particular situation in which this has come up is a proposal to update `_datetime_to_rfc3339()` to handle time zones as in the following module-private helper function appearing in #1691:

```
def _format_timestamp(timestamp):
    """Convert a datetime object to a string as required by the API.

    :type timestamp: :class:`datetime.datetime`
    :param timestamp: A datetime object.

    :rtype: string
    :returns: The formatted timestamp. For example:
        ``"2016-02-17T19:18:01.763000Z"``
    """
    if timestamp.tzinfo is not None:
        # Convert to UTC and remove the time zone info.
        timestamp = timestamp.replace(tzinfo=None) - timestamp.utcoffset()

    return timestamp.isoformat() + 'Z'
```

issue_title: Compatibility argument on `python_binary` target does not get piped through to PEX-INFO when target has no sources
issue_body:
The interpreter compatibility argument on the `python_binary` target does not get added to the pex builder object during the `python_binary_create` task when the target does not have python sources. An example of this is when the target specifies a dependency and sets an entry point in that dependency (ex: a `__main__.py` file of a directory containing a `python_library` target.)

The fix would be to eliminate the prereq of `has_python_sources` for a target before processing its compatibility constraints in the `python_binary_create` task.

issue_body:
This appears to have started with [this commit](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/commit/3882ecd39444665b56c842674bbadf0bb1da7a76) in [this CI run](https://travis-ci.org/pantsbuild-osx/pants/builds/87527470).

issue_title: javadoc is confused by jars on its classpath that contain scala class files
issue_body:
We ran into an issue where the default doclet generator provided with java8 fails to create the constant-values page in javadoc if there is a scala target's jar on the classpath that shares a package with one of the java files being acted on.

One solution would be to exclude internal non-java containing targets from javadoc generation tasks from the classpath used by javadoc.

issue_title: doc: options reference missing a bunch
issue_body:
Wondering whether this newfangled "advanced option" stuff would mess up options_reference, I looked to see how it shows compile.java's --target and --source.

Except I didn't: it doesn't show compile.java at all.

Stu had a great thought earlier which was essentially that in order to avoid daemon lifecycle invalidation when the string values of  `--pants-config-files` or `--pantsrc-files` change, we should not fingerprint these (because their transitive inner values will already be considered during fingerprinting and may or may not be affected by pointing to a new config or rc filename).

When use python_requirements, it will retrieve libriaries dependencies when running ./pants test or binary etc. Then it may retrieve different versions of dependencies in different build machine, which introduced uncertenty.

For python_library, it is ok, otherwise we may bitten by mismatch versions of depedencies from different libraries.
For python_binary, it should produce deterministic build.

Any idea?

```
>> 
from prometheus_client.parser import text_string_to_metric_families

usaddress.parse('JASON, BOURNE, 90 CLAIRE GULCH, CLANCY MT 59634-9528 ')

> +1 on duplicating button

### User-facing consequences

can lead to a confusing user experience

### Steps to reproduce

create a lesson from a channel with a topic that has many items? or is it create a very long lesson?

### Context

k08x

issue_title: Sample WARC shows two URIs available but only lists 1
issue_body: Though a second `<li>` is in the HTML.

Looking at the routing code in the `replay.py` it looks like the query string of URI-Rs is not considered wherever `<path:urir>` is matched. This means when looking up for the resource `http://example.com/foo?bar=baz`, the response will be returned for `http://example.com/foo` instead.

Note: This report is based on the quick look at the code. I have not tested it.

issue_title: Java OMX library won’t compile against HDF5 1.10
issue_body:
@jeabraham - I’m trying to use the Java OMX library and it won’t compile against HDF5 1.10 because they’ve moved to long pointers.  Has anyone tackled the conversion/update?  I’m giving it a go but it’s a bit of work.  

My motivation is actually to put the PECAS economic flow matrices (700 or so of them) into one file that can be read by a visualization program.  That visualization program is currently in Javascript.  Has anyone tackled reading OMX matrices from Javascript?  There’s a Javascript HDF5 library. See #32

issue_title: apt package is deprecated but not made clear
issue_body:
The `charm` apt package has been long deprecated in favor of the snap, but it has not been updated in any way.  Thus, users installing the apt package end up with a very old, now partially broken command.

We should update the apt package to install the snap, or at the very least print a message indicating that it is deprecated in favor of the snap and is out of date upon installation.

issue_title: pytz required but not in setup.py
issue_body:
If you install `mangopaysdk` via pip in a fresh virtualenv, and then do a `import mangopay`, it will complain that `pytz` is not found. So I assume it should be put in `setup.py` as well?

This occurred to me on Python 3.6.4, with only `mangopaysdk` and `ipython` installed via pip.

client.download takes a callback hook to report on downloading, but it's only called initially and during file write. Here's a test:

```python
from planet import api
client = api.ClientV1()

item_type='PSScene4Band'
item_id = '20170518_030019_1007'
asset_type='analytic_dn'
asset_type='basic_analytic_dn_xml_nitf'

assets = client.get_assets_by_id(item_type, id=item_id).get()

print('activating asset')
while assets[asset_type]['status']!='active':
    time.sleep(30)
    assets = client.get_assets(item).get()
    print(t0-time.time(),asset_type,assets[asset_type]['status'])
print('done')

def callback(*args,**kwargs):
    """Dummy callback that prints any inputs"""
    print('callback',args,kwargs)
req=client.download(assets[asset_type], callback=callback)

print('downloading body')
body=req.get_body()
# """
# ('callback', (<planet.api.models.Body object at 0x7f955c3b1890>,), {})
# """

print('writing')
body.write('/tmp/temp2.tif',callback=callback)
# """
# ('callback', (), {'start': <planet.api.models.Body object at 0x7f955c3a7b50>})
# ('callback', (), {'total': 8912, 'wrote': 8912})
# ('callback', (), {'finish': <planet.api.models.Body object at 0x7f955c3a7b50>})
# """
```

I think that:
-  `req.get_body()` should pass the callback to `req._dispatcher._dispatch` and `req._dispatcher._dispatch_async`
- _dispatch and _dispatch_async are already fine
- _do_request should pass it to [`sess.request` ](https://github.com/planetlabs/planet-client-python/blob/fd74e39d970325e9db8d87ce129e480981d82084/planet/api/dispatch.py#L113)

issue_title: epdb.serve() does not work on python 3.x

issue_title: nimodinst.Session is indexed directly rather than through repeated capability container
issue_body:
It appears we didn't introduce repeated capability container to nimodinst as part of #718. This is inconsistent. I suggest we turn `session[0].chassis_number` into `session.devices[0].chassis_number`.

It's more typing but it's also more consistent with the other nimi-python APIs.

There is an [issue](http://bugs.python.org/issue29082) in Python 2.7.13 where _ctypes.LoadLibrary no longer accepts Unicode objects. This will be patched in 2.7.14, but for now users should downgrade to 2.7.12 to use the **nidaqmx** package.

The calibration portion of the DAQmx API is not supported.

See http://zone.ni.com/reference/en-XX/help/370471AA-01/TOC32.htm

virtualenv test

https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/1700db4aa9323adb156cd87fd7bd9bba5b92532c/allennlp/nn/util.py#L463-L475

Due to the mesos.py conflicting with the mesos python library, the mesos-collector doesn't work as expected.

Workaround: rename mesos.py to meso.py

I found it when I kept getting
`1444659840.74   [MainProcess:27359:ERROR]       Can not find collector MesosCollector`

After adding some debug printing of what was actually being loaded, I saw
`<module 'mesos' (built-in)>`
and not
`<module 'mesos' from '/usr/share/diamond/collectors/mesos/mesos.py'>`
As I was expecting.

Hello @Gaurav Jain, @josegonzalez, @kormoc, @mattrobenolt, @mzupan, @rtoma, @shortdudey123 (I'm picking on you all simply because you're listed on https://github.com/orgs/python-diamond/people :)),

 The last commit to land in this repo happened way back in May 2017, not far off a year ago.

The most recent release, 4.0.515, was made in November 2016, which was about a year and 3 months ago.

A backlog of issues and PRs have built up and it doesn't look like the majority of these have had a response from any of you guys.

Is there any particular reason for this? Should I start thinking about migrating to a different stats collector such as collectd?

I've enjoyed using diamond so far and it essentially does everything I need it to do. I recently put out #698 and I'm also looking at contributing a new Kafka handler, but I don't want to spend the time doing this if no-one's going to review my code.

I completely understand that you'll be working on many other projects, both open-source and for work (or a hybrid), I'm in a similar position, however if there's anything I can do in terms of helping maintain this code, I'd be happy to help, although with the main caveat being I'm more interested in the infra code, rather than any specific collector or handler.

Thanks,
David

issue_title: Behaviour of direct assignment of reverse ForeignKeys
issue_body:
At the moment, code like this:
```
from django.db import models
from versions.models import Versionable, VersionedForeignKey


class Beer(Versionable):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=50)


class BeerDrinker(Versionable):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    now_drinking = VersionedForeignKey(Beer, null=True, blank=True, related_name='drinkers')

beer1 = Beer.objects.create(name="Chopsfab Hell")
drinker1 = BeerDrinker.objects.create(name="Joe", now_drinking=beer1)
drinker2 = BeerDrinker.objects.create(name="Mary", now_drinking=beer1)

t0 = get_utc_now()
beer1 = beer1.clone()
beer1.drinkers = []
print len(Beer.objects.as_of(t0).drinkers.all())
```

will print "0" - which seems wrong, because there were two drinkers at time t0.

I'm not sure how this should be handled at the moment.  Two ideas:
* At direct assignment time, automatically clone the referencing records
* At clone time, also clone referencing records

Does anyone else have some input / ideas?

issue_title: Deis should send host header with health checks.
issue_body: Many apps validate the "Host:" header as a security measure (e.g. Django does this by default). As such health check pings using just the IP will return a 400 unless the IP is specifically added to the config. The various health checks should therefore add a host header to the requests with the value being the FQDN for the app (e.g. `Host: friendly-dino.example.com`) so that apps can continue simply including the expected domains in the configuration.

issue_title: store: Consider using ceph-fuse rather than the kernel client for Ceph FS (deis-store-volume)
issue_body:
When Ceph fixes bugs in the Linux kernel driver for Ceph FS, we have to wait for the patch to get accepted, a kernel to be released, and then a CoreOS release with that kernel version to make it to the stable channel.

Instead of using the kernel client, we could instead use [ceph-fuse](http://ceph.com/docs/master/man/8/ceph-fuse/), which allows us to upgrade to the latest any time the container is rebuilt. If we go this route, we would certainly have to test upgrades from the kernel client filesystem to a FUSE filesystem.

I'm hoping this will prevent Ceph bugs from taking down instances (which @ianblenke has seen), but it definitely allows us to quickly adopt upstream fixes (specifically [this one](http://lists.ceph.com/pipermail/ceph-users-ceph.com/2015-January/045838.html) reported by @lorieri, which has a patch pending).

issue_title: Improve error message for python2.6 installs
issue_body:
Right now, if someone attempts to install on py2 < py2.7 , the version loading will run and blow up on a format string.

This presents as a SyntaxError, and makes it hard (for the uninitiated) to recognize the cause.
It should be easy enough for us to check, at the start of `setup.py`, whether or not we're on an older python2 and exit with a nicer error message.

We can't do very much about the way that pip/setuptools will present that message in the midst of an install though.

issue_title: Add `total_latency_ms` and `queue_length` metric for Unity LUN.
issue_body:
Add Unity LUN metrics `total_latency_ms` and `queue_length`.

Related paths:
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.totalIoTime
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.currentIOCount
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.reads
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.writes

Formula:
* Total latency (ms):
>  ((spa totalIoTime) + (spb totalTime)) / ((spa total calls) + (spb total calls))/1000
* Average Queue Length for each SP (count):
>  delta(currentIOCount) * (delta(busyTime)/(delta(idleTime) + delta(busyTime)))
* Average Queue Length (count): 
> ((spa Queue Length) * (spa total calls) + (spb Queue Length) * (spb total calls)) / ((spa total calls) + (spb total calls))

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zero_crossing

could be an interesting, easy to add feature

issue_title: SQL Server: crash on conn.close() without rollback/commit
issue_body:
When closing a connection (only after cursor usage), I get the following error:

```
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'cpp_odbc::error'
  what():  ODBC error
state: 25000
native error code: 0
message: [Microsoft][ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server]Invalid transaction state
```

Script to reproduce the error:
```python
import turbodbc

conn = turbodbc.connect(driver='ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server', 
    server='xxx', port='1433', uid='xxx', pwd='xxx')
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM sys.tables")
cursor.close()
conn.close()
```

This can be prevented by calling `conn.rollback()` or `conn.commit()` before closing the connection, since turbodbc connects to SQL Server in manual commit mode, which means all commands will be running inside of a transaction as soon as you open a cursor, and closing a connection before closing the transaction will cause this error.

Specifications:
Fedora 25 - 64 bit
Python 3.5.2
Turbodbc 1.0.0
unixODBC 2.3.4
ODBC Driver: msodbcsql-13.1.4.0-1 (official Microsoft driver)

issue_title: Latin Extended-A characters being converted to ASCII on INSERT
issue_body:
If I insert a Latin Extended-A character into my MS SQL Server database, it appears to be getting converted to its ASCII equivalent character.

Here's my setup:
Python 3.4.4, running on CentOS 6.6
MS SQL Server 2008 R2
Using the Microsoft ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server on Linux with unixODBC 2.3.2

```sql
create table dbo.turbodbc (
  col_int int null,
  col_str_a varchar(100) null,  -- ascii (single-byte per character) column
  col_str_u nvarchar(100) null  -- unicode (actually UCS2 LE, two bytes per character) column
)
```
```python
# the following string has three test characters separated by spaces:
#   a normal "ascii" character c, hex value \x63
#   c-cedilla, from "Latin-1 Supplement" but still a one byte value \xE7
#   c-with-an-acute-accent, from "Latin Extended-A", i.e. needs two bytes, value \x0107
# see http://inamidst.com/stuff/unidata/ for reference
string = 'c \xe7 \u0107'
row = [1, string, string]
sql = "insert into dbo.turbodbc (col_int, col_str_a, col_str_u) values (?, ?, ?)"
crsr = conn.cursor()
crsr.execute(sql, row)
conn.commit()
```
If I query for the hex equivalent of the two columns in the table, I get: `0x6320E72063` and `0x63002000E70020006300`.  As you can see, the third test character has been converted from c-with-an-acute-accent to plain old c, in both columns. I can kind-of understand this would happen in the first (ascii) column because the column can contain only single-byte characters, but the second "unicode" column should be able to handle this and hence contain `0x63002000E70020000701`, i.e. end with `0701` not `6300` (note the little-endianness).

Somewhere along the line, the "Latin Extended-A" character \u0107 is being translated to \u0063, even if the target column is a unicode column.  Would this be happening within turbodbc?  Or somewhere else?  Is turbodbc designed to work only with single-byte characters?

`cursor.executemanycolumns()` supports `datetime64[us]` and `datetime64[D]` columns (https://github.com/blue-yonder/turbodbc/blob/master/cpp/turbodbc_numpy/Library/src/set_numpy_parameters.cpp#L301). Support should be extended to `datetime64[ns]`, which is the type used by `pandas` for timestamp columns. This would allow pandas users to directly pass such columns as parameters without prior conversion to `datetime64[us]`.

This issue captures the essence of #112.

I chose 24 as the value for that constant but that's an arbitrary value as I couldn't find out any info about how long chain reorgs tend to be. We should reconsider that value, specially if we start seeing aborted peer syncs because we cannot find a common ancestor

### What is wrong?

In the `trinity` module we have a number of path objects that are typed as `str`.  

The `pathlib` standard library module has a [`PurePath`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html#pathlib.PurePath) class which is more appropriate.


### How can it be fixed

Update the `trinity` module to use the `PurePath` type for all filesystem paths.  If it seems appropriate, lets also update the code to actually use and return `PurePath` objects.

### What is wrong?

If a transaction fails after PAYGAS has been called, the sender ends up with his initial balance plus some additional funds. The reason for this seems to be that at the point of failure, the state is reverted to the pre gas payment state (i.e. the original balance), and on top of that the unused gas is refunded.

Here's the test case that discovered the bug: https://github.com/jannikluhn/py-evm/blob/de5365458c1839a47a1301551e3b62e54c5214ef/tests/fillers/vm_fillers/paygas_fillers.py#L322-L349

In this case, the account at `address` ends up with a balance of `138958` instead of `38958`.

### How can it be fixed

After reverting the state subtract the transaction fee again

Ping @NIC619

### What is wrong?
After the old codes are removed, we can start to implement the `Shard` class which is similar to `Chain` class.

### How can it be fixed
Implementing `evm/chains/shard.py` and it's NOT an inheritance/implementation of the `BaseChain` now.

### What is wrong?

Testing networking services in isolation is quite difficult right now, as discussed in #688 .

### How can it be fixed

I think we should try to separate different layers of the code more so that we can replace them easily. Here's a concrete proposal:

- Each `BasePeer` gets a `BasePeerInterface` instance. `BasePeer` calls `BasePeerInterface.send_command(command)`, `BasePeerInterface` calls
 `BasePeer.process_command(command)` (alternatively, both classes have a `command_queue` on which they put commands on).
- To allow this, `Command`s keep parameters as instance variables.
- `StreamPeerInterface` (or just `PeerInterface`) writes commands to the output stream and reads them from the input stream, just like the current `BasePeer` does.
- For tests, there's a `MockPeerInterface` that passes commands through to a shared `Network` object that sends them to the receiving `MockPeerInterface`. That means that nothing goes over a network interface and nothing needs to be encoded or decoded.
- Add a `MockDiscovery` which gets a list of peer pools to "discover".
- `PeerPool` makes the `handshake` function configurable, either by taking it as a constructor argument or by making a `BasePeerPool` and requiring it to implement it in sub classes. We add a `mock_handshake` that looks up the remote in `Network` and creates a new peer with a `MockPeerInterface`.

I haven't thought this through completely (especially how it affects `PeerPool` and `Server`), but don't have more time just now and wanted to write it down. Will update this tonight when I'll hopefully had some more time.

issue_title: Problem with icons and omitted underspecified variable
issue_body:
There is a problem with omitted underspecified variables i and icons that refer to them. The sentence **_"Impressed by the cat, Kim laughed."_** should produce the following EP:
`[ _impress_v_1_rel<0:9> LBL: h7 ARG0: e8 [ e SF: prop TENSE: untensed MOOD: indicative ] ARG1: x9 ARG2: i10 ]`
Instead, it omits ARG2, but the icons list subsequently refers to it:
`ICONS: < e8 topic i10 >`

The same with the 1214 version, where icons are handled differently:
`[ _impress_v_1_rel<0:9> LBL: h7 ARG0: e8 [ e SF: prop TENSE: untensed MOOD: indicative ] ARG1: x9 ARG2: i10 ]
[ parg_d_rel LBL: h7 ARG0: e22 [ e SF: prop ] ARG1: e8 ARG2: i10 ]`

Overall, this should probably be parsed differently anyway, with i10 refering to the variable representing the subject of the main clause instead.

The command-line scripts `pyDelphin` and `mrs.py` would be better distributed as an "entry point" defined in setup.py.

See here: http://pythonhosted.org/setuptools/setuptools.html#automatic-script-creation

See how the Xigt project does it:
  - https://github.com/xigt/xigt/blob/master/setup.py#L90
  - https://github.com/xigt/xigt/blob/master/xigt/main.py
As a beta and undocumented feature for future use, add a configurable subdirs parameter.

issue_title: conda remove doesn't complain if only some of the packages don't exist
issue_body:
If I do `conda remove foo bar` and neither of them are in the current environment then I get an error.  However if I do `conda remove foo bar` and `foo` is present but `bar` is not then `foo` gets removed but there is no error or warning that `bar` wasn't there and was not removed.  I discovered this because I misunderstood the behavior of the `--features` flag, so it also applies to removing features.

I would suggest that a warning is required as a minimum, and perhaps it should be an error to be "symmetric" with a `conda install foo bar` where `foo` exists to be installed but `bar` does not (which just reports an error, and does not install anything).

issue_body:
The `ssl_verify` option in conda doesn't seem to work in conda 4.4.8+. (I haven't tested preceding versions, just 4.4.8 and 4.4.10, but it certainly used to work some time ago.)

I have the option enabled currently:

```none
> conda config --get ssl_verify

--set ssl_verify False
```

And yet:

```none
> conda update foo

An HTTP error occurred when trying to retrieve this URL.
HTTP errors are often intermittent, and a simple retry will get you on your way.
SSLError(MaxRetryError('HTTPSConnectionPool(host=\'an-internal-server\', port=443):
Max retries exceeded with url: /repository/some-repo/win-64/repodata.json
(Caused by SSLError(SSLError("bad handshake: Error([(\'SSL routines\', \'ssl3_get_server_certificate\', \'certificate verify failed\')],)",),))',),)
```

If I access this same URL using `requests` directly instead then it's fine:

```python
url = "https://an-internal-server/repository/some-repo/win-64/repodata.json"
requests.get(url, verify=False)
<Response [200]>
```

This may be Windows-specific, as it seems fine on my Mac with identical configuration. Here's the user agent string reported by `conda info`:

```

issue_title: ctable.append fails when argument is single row from itself
issue_body:
The append method cannot accept the return value of it's own accessor, for a single row.

This fails:

```python
example_table.append(example_table[0])
```

while this works:

```python
example_table.append(example_table[0:1])
```

This fails because the type of the return value is `numpy.void` instead of `numpy.ndarray`, 
the, the check is on this line in ctable: [ctable.py#L354](https://github.com/Blosc/bcolz/blob/bb148ffaf9a035d71cbd004d07d59ba66800e019/bcolz/ctable.py#L354)

Hi,

I have been using pysam library to process, filter and merge BAM files, but I have been facing some performance issues. Comparing to a script using python subprocess module running `samtools` on shell, I get about twice faster results. Here's a single example trial:

    ~/D/w/tmp ❯❯❯ time python3 subprocess_reader.py
    87329 reads were removed.
    python3 subprocess_reader.py 6,56s user 0,13s system 58% cpu 11,410 total

    87329 reads were removed.
    python3 pysam_filter.py 13,82s user 0,14s system 97% cpu 14,324 total

I understand the library must have some overhead to organize the reads information, but I don't believe it explains the difference of almost 2x. Any advice? Am I doing anything stupid maybe?

Follows the code for both alternatives:

_subprocess_

    with open('temp_.bam', 'wb') as outstream:

issue_title: Inconsistent htslib version dependencies in docs
issue_body:
There are a bunch of places in the pysam documentation where the htslib version that `pysam 0.13.0` depends on is specified. 

According to:

- the [release notes](https://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/release.html?highlight=htslib) it's `htslib 1.6.0`
- the [main readthedocs page](https://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) it's `htslib 1.3.0`
- the [pypi page](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pysam/0.13) it's `htslib 1.4.1`

I can confirm that `pysam 0.13.0` fails to compile with `htslib 1.4.1` if I link it manually against a homebrew install version on my mac:
```fish
set -x HTSLIB_LIBRARY_DIR /usr/local/Cellar/htslib/1.4.1/lib 
set -x HTSLIB_INCLUDE_DIR /usr/local/Cellar/htslib/1.4.1/include
pip install pysam
```

, which fails with
```

issue_title: functional coverage is not merged in Modelsim coverage merge
issue_body:
While using Modelsim/Questasim, the final merged coverage file is missing the functional coverage despite that each of the individual testcase coverage has it.

I found that this is because of the option "-strip 1"  in vcover merge (in vunit-master/vunit/modelsim_interface.py). Once I removed this option, I can see the functional covergroups in the merged coverage file.

Basically, the "-strip 1" option excludes all the coverage information outside the DUT, i.e the testbench. But, functional coverage is by definition a part of the testbench, unlike code coverage in the DUT.

Therefore, this option needs to be removed or configurable.
If the user wants to suppress coverage information for certain scopes/files, they can do it using disable_coverage instead.

issue_title: Reindex of an empty list using REINDEX_SELECTION causes massive reindex
issue_body:
### Expected Behavior
In a reindex action, If there is no index in the list after the filtering stage, curator should raise an exception and don't send the task to elasticsearch. In addition it should give the option to ignore that exception using `ignore_empty_list: true` but the reindex shouldn't be executed 

### Actual Behavior
Elasticsearch reindex api seems to accept an empty list of indexes as source and it takes it like a `*` (all the indexes)
The reindex task is sent to elasticsearch an it tries to run the reindex using all the indexes as source. Hopefully, the task will fail with a validation error but I think it's really dangerous to depend only on Elasticsearch's validation

### Steps to Reproduce the Problem
1 - Create an action like:

```
  1:
    description: "Reindex last day's index into monthly"
    action: reindex
    options:
      ignore_empty_list: true
      requests_per_second: 5000
      wait_interval: 30
      max_wait:      -1
      request_body:
        source:
          index: REINDEX_SELECTION
        dest:
          index: monthly-2018.01
    filters:
      - filtertype: pattern
        kind: prefix
        value: asdasd-
```

2 - Ensure that the filter doesn't match any index

3 - Debug output of curator on action execution:
```
...
2018-01-31 21:26:49,309 DEBUG              curator.utils        iterate_filters:1087 Post-instance: []
2018-01-31 21:26:49,309 DEBUG     curator.actions.reindex               __init__:1055 REQUEST_BODY = {'dest': {'index': 'monthly-2018.01'}, 'source': {'index': 'REINDEX_SELECTION'}}
2018-01-31 21:26:49,309 DEBUG     curator.actions.reindex               __init__:1228 Reindexing indices: []
2018-01-31 21:26:49,309 DEBUG                curator.cli         process_action:99   Doing the action here.
2018-01-31 21:26:49,309 INFO      curator.actions.reindex              do_action:1335 Commencing reindex operation
2018-01-31 21:26:49,309 DEBUG     curator.actions.reindex              do_action:1337 REINDEX: request body: {'dest': {'index': 'monthly-2018.01'}, 'source': {'index': []}} with arguments: refresh=True requests_per_second=5000 slices=1 timeout=60s wait_for_active_shards=1 wait_for_completion=True
2018-01-31 21:26:49,666 ERROR                curator.cli                    run:184  Failed to complete action: reindex.  <class 'curator.exceptions.FailedExecution'>: Exception encountered.  Rerun with loglevel DEBUG and/or check Elasticsearch logs for more information. Exception: TransportError(400, u'action_request_validation_exception', u'Validation Failed: 1: use _all if you really want to copy from all existing indexes;')
```

### Specifications

  - Version: Curator 5.4.1
  - Platform: Docker container with base image `python:2.7-alpine`
  - Elasticsearch version `5.5.0`

## Detailed Description
If you have a reindex action using `REINDEX_SELECTION` as source index, and a couple of filters on that action filtering out every index (i.e returning an empty list), then elasticsearch is instructed to perform a reindex from `[]` to `dest-index`.

This doesn't seem the expected behavior on using filters. If no index match the filters, no task should executed
See [my question here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29964324/is-rolling-indices-possible-with-an-atomic-alias-move-for-the-curator-from-ela)

I'm running perfectly in python 2.6

But running on 3.6 I got this error:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "criaPicos.py", line 2, in <module>

issue_title: Transparently handle SAS key when working with blobs
issue_body: Having to manage multiple authentication levels with the CLI seems a little excessive.  Why not just translate the current user's credentials for them into whatever access is required to perform the operation?

IIUC, `az storage blob delete` will delete just one blob.

Similar to #998, it would be nice to be able to delete a whole
"directory".  It looks like the only way to do that currently is to get
a list of all blobs using `--prefix` then iterate over each one of them,
which would overall be extremely slow compared to a single
`delete-batch`.

Please update example in `az vm delete --help`

Currently in azure-cli (2.0.1):
```
Examples
    Delete a VM without a prompt for confirmation.
        az vm delete -g MyResourceGroup -n MyVm --force
```
Should be
```
Examples
    Delete a VM without a prompt for confirmation.
        az vm delete -g MyResourceGroup -n MyVm --yes
```

I found that even the `get_object` case was leaking in aiobotocore.  So I tried doing a the underlying GET aiohttp request and found it triggers a leak in aiohttp.

## Expected behaviour

no leak

## Actual behaviour

leaking ~800kB over 300 requests

## Steps to reproduce

update the below script with your credentials and run like so: `PYTHONMALLOC=malloc /valgrind/bin/python3 /valgrind/bin/mprof run --interval=1 /tmp/test_aio.py head_object`


```python

import gc

import re

CREDENTIAL = put cred here

    'https://s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/archpi.dabase.com/style.css': {
        'method': 'get',
        'headers': {'User-Agent': 'Botocore/1.8.21 Python/3.6.4 Darwin/17.5.0', 'X-Amz-Date': '20180518T025044Z', 'X-Amz-Content-SHA256': 'e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855', 'Authorization': f'AWS4-HMAC-SHA256 Credential={CREDENTIAL}/20180518/us-west-2/s3/aws4_request, SignedHeaders=host;x-amz-content-sha256;x-amz-date, Signature=ae552641b9aa9a7a267fcb4e36960cd5863e55d91c9b45fd39b30fdcd2e81489', 'Accept-Encoding': 'identity'}
    },

    'https://s3.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/archpi.dabase.com/doesnotexist': {
        'method': 'GET' if sys.argv[1] == 'get_object' else 'HEAD',
        'headers': {'User-Agent': 'Botocore/1.8.21 Python/3.6.4 Darwin/17.5.0', 'X-Amz-Date': '20180518T025221Z', 'X-Amz-Content-SHA256': 'e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855', 'Authorization': f'AWS4-HMAC-SHA256 Credential={CREDENTIAL}/20180518/ap-southeast-1/s3/aws4_request, SignedHeaders=host;x-amz-content-sha256;x-amz-date, Signature=7a7675ef6d70cb647ed59e02d532ffa80d437fb03976d8246ea9ef102d118794', 'Accept-Encoding': 'identity'}
    }
}


async def test(s3_client):

async def test_aiohttp(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, url):

            data = await response.read()
    except:
        pass


async def do_get_obj(s3_client):
    response = await s3_client.get_object(Bucket='archpi.dabase.com', Key='style.css')
    async with response["Body"] as stream:
        data = await response["Body"].read()


async def aioboto_test():
    boto_session = aiobotocore.session.get_session()
    boto_config = botocore.config.Config(connect_timeout=15, read_timeout=15, max_pool_connections=1)

        print(".", end='', flush=True)

async def aiohttp_test():
    conn = aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit=1)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=conn) as session:
        await test_aiohttp(session, 'https://s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/archpi.dabase.com/style.css')

            re._cache.clear()
            gc.collect()
            print(".", end='', flush=True)
            if time.time() - start > (60 * 5):

async def main():
    await aiohttp_test()


if __name__ == '__main__':

If servers are hosted on Amazon DNS caches first answered IP.
In its turn Amazon uses round-robin to iterate over all IPs registered in load balancer.

Therefore aiohttp with cache enabled sends all payload to only one server from registered group.

The solution might don't use DNS cache for threaded DNS resolver and respect TTL returned by server for `aiodns` based implementation.

To implement the feature the moving DNS caching into resolver makes sense.

issue_title: allow partial payload to be returned
issue_body:
## Long story short

I'm querying a server that seems to be buggy; it returns a Content-Length header that is longer than the actual response, and the server disconnects once it has sent the response body.  The aiohttp client throws the following error when I try to read the response:
aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientPayloadError: Response payload is not completed

## Expected behaviour

There should be a way to get at the payload that's been read.  For example, curl outputs a useful warning but still returns the full body.

## Actual behaviour

This error is raised:
aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientPayloadError: Response payload is not completed
and there is no way (as far as I can tell) to get the body.

## Steps to reproduce

Do a GET request from a server that returns a body and closes the connection, after sending a Content-Length header that is larger than the actual body it sends.

## Your environment

Python 3.6.4, aiohttp 3.2.1.

New hierarchy of exceptions:

- `ClientError`
  - `ClientResponseError` - exceptions that could happen after we get response from server 
     - `WSServerHandshakeError` - web socket server response error
     - `ClientHttpProxyError` - proxy response
  - `ClientConnectionError` - exceptions related to low-level connection problem
     - `ClientOSError` - subset of connection errors that are initiated by OSError exception
        - `ClientConnectorError` - connector related exceptions
           - ClientProxyConnectionError` - proxy connection initialization error
     - `ServerConnectionError` - server connection related errors
        - `ServerDisconnectedError` - server disconnected
        - `ServerTimeoutError` - server operation timeout, (read timeout, etc)
        - `ServerFingerprintMismatch` - server fingerprint mistmach

I have run through the instructions for Getting Started with the Raspberry Pi and Python page for the Google Assistant SDK. issue_title: matplotlib plot does not fully render if an exception is raised before cell execution completes
issue_body:
I run the following in a notebook cell:
```
%matplotlib notebook
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def plot_something():
    plt.figure()
    plt.plot([0,2],[0,2],'-')
    plt.show()

plot_something()
raise Exception
```

Expected output: 
- an interactive figure created with the line x==y on range [0,2]
- exception output (including a traceback).

Actual output:
- an interactive figure is created, but the line is not rendered/visible
- exception output (including traceback).

![notebook_matplotlib_exception](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/721123/22779232/9821297e-eec2-11e6-872b-806b7d83a04d.jpg)

Looking at the x,y prompt on the bottom right of the figure widget (attached screenshot) you can see that the axis range was set to [0,2] for both x & y. I have verified that by changing the plot parameters and checking that the top right corner x,y values change accordingly.

This issue is problematic when a cell executes a calculation which creates multiple figures. Any exception raised within the cell will prevent any plot from properly rendering, including the plots created before the exception was raised.

The issue is also exacerbated when the exception is raised in a following cell which gets invoked before the cell creating the plots completes execution. This is described in more detail in the [SO question I posted](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/40123739/force-matplotlib-to-fully-render-plots-in-an-ipython-jupyter-notebook-cell-befor).

I am not sure whether this belongs here or on matplotlib issues. I was unable to find a duplicate for this searching the issues for both.

Thanks!
